original_abstract:
We introduce a novel large-scale dataset, Microsoft Common Objects in Context (MS COCO), designed to advance object recognition by situating it within the broader challenge of scene understanding. While existing datasets often focus on iconic views of objects, MS COCO targets the detection and localization of common objects in their natural, complex environments. The dataset contains 328,000 images depicting 91 easily recognizable object categories, with a total of 2.5 million labeled instances. 

A key distinguishing feature of MS COCO is the use of per-instance segmentation masks for every labeled object, providing more precise spatial localization than standard bounding boxes. To construct this resource, we developed an extensive crowdsourcing pipeline that utilizes novel user interfaces for category labeling, instance spotting, and fine-grained segmentation. Statistical analysis demonstrates that MS COCO contains significantly more object instances per image compared to PASCAL VOC and ImageNet, effectively capturing the contextual relationships between objects. Finally, we provide baseline performance benchmarks for object detection and segmentation using Deformable Part Models, highlighting the increased difficulty of recognizing objects in non-canonical views and cluttered scenes.
humanized_abstract:
Publication Volume 14 Issue 9 doi 10.1561/42000000163 Introduction Object detection research has been limited by several empirical and theoretical drawbacks with current datasets such as large number of classes, limitations in object viewpoints, diversity in scenes, small data sizes, insufficient variations in pose and location, ambiguity of object boundaries, reliance on human-drawn labels, absence of person-centered perspectives, and lack of rigorous evaluation protocols. While image classification, object categorization, face and pedestrian detection are all important parts of scene understanding, detection of common objects in realistic scenes remains an unfulfilled research goal. MS COCO is a newly published large-scale dataset providing a large set of images, with a large set of objects annotated with accurate localization information. MS COCO contains 328,000 images with 91 common object classes, on which 2.5 million object instances have been labeled.

Previous studies on object detection and segmentation have heavily relied on popular benchmarks such as PASCAL VOC and ImageNet. However, we argue that due to limited number of labeled instances in these datasets, objects in test images may appear far more frequently in the training set in non-canonical poses and in more cluttered scenes. Thus, there is an urgent need for a more robust object detection and segmentation benchmark. Unlike previous benchmarks that assign a bounding box to each object, we use per-instance segmentation masks for every object in MS COCO. We have built this dataset by developing a fully automated crowdsourcing platform using few novel interfaces to annotate objects in various categories. Our analysis shows that there are more object instances per image in MS COCO than in any other widely used datasets like PASCAL VOC and ImageNet which reflects a deeper understanding of the environment around objects. We present the object detection and segmentation performance on our new benchmark for the state-of-the-art Deformable Part Models (DPMs). Our evaluations confirm our conjecture that the novel aspect of our dataset results in highly challenging object detection and segmentation performances.

document.input:
We introduce a novel large-scale dataset, Microsoft Common Objects in Context (MS COCO), designed to advance object recognition by situating it within the broader challenge of scene understanding. While existing datasets often focus on iconic views of objects, MS COCO targets the detection and localization of common objects in their natural, complex environments. Finally, we provide baseline performance benchmarks for object detection and segmentation using Deformable Part Models, highlighting the increased difficulty of recognizing objects in non-canonical views and cluttered scenes.
